perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario and the laws of Canada applicable therein and herby submit to the juris diction of the courts of the Provi nce of Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Canada.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit mention of the Province of Ontario and the submission to the jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Ontario, the location of jurisdiction for legal disputes arising from this agreement becomes ambiguous. This increases complexity in determining which specific provincial laws apply and where a lawsuit must be filed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 16
perturbation.law_citation: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 16
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 16 of the Rules of Civil Procedure in Ontario outlines the rules for determining the place of trial. Omitting the clause that the parties submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Ontario makes the appropriate venue for litigation less clear, potentially leading to disputes about jurisdiction and forum, increasing costs and complexity.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Ontario law and jurisdiction, whereas the changed text only specifies Canadian law. The Law Explanation states omitting the clause about submitting to Ontario jurisdiction makes the venue for litigation less clear and increases complexity. The Perturbation Explanation says this increases complexity in determining which specific provincial laws apply. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Rules of Civil Procedure which are impacted by this change. Removing Ontario's jurisdiction introduces ambiguity and possible legal challenges that did not exist in the original agreement, creating a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any demand, notice or other communication to be given in connection with this agreement shall be given in writing and shall be given by personal delivery, by registered mail or by electronic means of communication addressed to Recipient a s follows: Investigator : [NAME OF DEPARTMENT] [NAME OF BUILDING] [ROOM NUMBER] 1125 Colonel By Drive Ottawa, ON K1S 5B6 Company : [NAME AND COORDINATES]
perturbation.changed_text: Any demand, notice or other communication to be given in connection with this agreement shall be given by personal delivery or by registered mail.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'electronic means of communication' as an accepted method for delivering notices, the agreement might contradict modern communication practices and legal standards regarding electronic communication, especially given the widespread use of email.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Commerce Act, 2000, S.O. 2000, c. 17, s. 22
perturbation.law_citation: Electronic Commerce Act, 2000, S.O. 2000, c. 17, s. 22
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 22 of the Electronic Commerce Act, 2000 states that unless otherwise agreed by the parties, an electronic document is deemed to be sent when it enters an information processing system outside the control of the originator, or if the originator and addressee use the same information system, when it becomes capable of being retrieved and processed by the addressee. By removing the option of electronic communication, the agreement potentially creates ambiguity on valid communication methods and conflicts with the provisions of the Electronic Commerce Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'electronic means of communication' as an acceptable method for delivering notices. The Law Explanation states that this omission 'potentially creates ambiguity on valid communication methods and conflicts with the provisions of the Electronic Commerce Act.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Section 22 of the Electronic Commerce Act, dealing with the 'Time of sending of electronic information or document'. By removing electronic communication, the agreement contradicts modern communication practices, creating a structural flaw by potentially conflicting with the Electronic Commerce Act regarding valid communication methods.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereto hereby acknowledge th at they have required this Agreement to be drawn up in English language.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereto hereby acknowledge this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the statement that the agreement was required to be drawn up in English, the agreement could be challenged based on language if one of the parties does not understand English, particularly in Quebec where French has specific protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Charter of the French Language, CQLR c C-11, s. 55
perturbation.law_citation: Charter of the French Language, CQLR c C-11, s. 55
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 55 of the Charter of the French Language generally requires contracts of adhesion to be drawn up in French, unless the adhering party expressly wishes the contract to be drawn up in another language. While this agreement is not necessarily a contract of adhesion, removing the clause that explicitly states that the agreement was drawn up in English could create ambiguity and a potential challenge if one of the parties is located in Quebec and does not have sufficient knowledge of English.

perturbation.justification: The original text includes a statement acknowledging that the agreement was drawn up in English. The changed text removes this statement. According to the 'Law Explanation,' removing this clause could create ambiguity and a potential challenge if one of the parties is located in Quebec and does not have sufficient knowledge of English, potentially conflicting with Section 55 of the Charter of the French Language. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 'C-11 - Charter of the French language', strengthening the claim that Quebec's language laws are relevant. The 'Perturbation Explanation' also indicates that this removal could lead to a challenge based on language, particularly in Quebec, because the removed clause addressed the language in which the agreement was drawn. This falls under 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction', since removing the statement about the language of the agreement introduces a structural flaw that weakens its enforceability under Quebec law.
